body_tmpl: Mayor, the annual life-safety inspection of {BIZ} at {ADDRESS} (inspection {CASE}) was completed on {DATE} with no outstanding deficiencies. Sprinkler and alarm systems carry current certifications, exit signage and emergency lighting per IFC 1013 and 1008 are operational, and the means of egress are clear per IFC 1031. Portable extinguishers are tagged and current under NFPA 10. The occupancy is compliant and the certificate of compliance will be mailed to the owner. No further action required from your office.
reply_tmpl: Good to hear. Thanks for the quick turnaround.

body_tmpl: Mayor, the annual inspection of {BIZ} at {ADDRESS} on {DATE} (inspection {CASE}) found two correctable deficiencies. First, a blocked rear egress door secured with a non-compliant deadbolt, contrary to IFC 1010.1.9 requiring single-action egress. Second, three portable extinguishers past their annual service tag under NFPA 10. Neither rises to an imminent-hazard closure. I issued a correction notice with a 30-day deadline and scheduled a re-inspection for the following month. Flagging for your awareness since {BIZ} is on the main corridor.
reply_tmpl: Thanks for the heads-up. Keep me posted on whether they correct by the deadline.

body_tmpl: Mayor, I completed the commercial cooking inspection at {BIZ}, {ADDRESS} on {DATE} (inspection {CASE}). The Type I hood and wet-chemical suppression system passed its semiannual service per NFPA 96, and the fusible links were replaced on schedule. The K-Class extinguisher is in place at the cook line per IFC 904.13. One note: grease accumulation in the exhaust duct was heavier than expected, and I advised the owner to move to quarterly cleaning given their fry volume. No violation issued — the system is compliant. Sharing for the record.

Mayor, the annual inspection of the {BIZ} apartments at {ADDRESS} on {DATE} (inspection {CASE}) turned up several fire-alarm deficiencies in the common areas. The building fire-alarm panel showed two zones in trouble, and battery-backup testing failed under NFPA 72. Smoke detection in the second-floor corridor was non-functional. I also documented one self-closing stairwell door propped open in violation of IFC 716. I issued a Notice of Violation with a 14-day correction window given the residential occupancy. The owner has been cooperative. Will update after re-inspection.
reply_tmpl: 14 days is right for occupied residential. Please loop in the building official if anything slips.

body_tmpl: Mayor, the annual Group E inspection at {BIZ}, {ADDRESS} was completed {DATE} ahead of the fall term (inspection {CASE}). Fire drills are documented on schedule, corridor egress and exit discharge are clear per IFC 1031, and the sprinkler system passed its annual flow and main-drain test under NFPA 25. One minor item: extension cords used in lieu of permanent wiring in two classrooms, contrary to IFC 605.5. Staff removed them on-site, so no formal notice was needed. The building is cleared for occupancy. Good cooperation from the principal and custodial staff.
reply_tmpl: Glad it's wrapped before the term starts. Thank you.

subject_tmpl: Notice of Violation issued — blocked egress, {BIZ}
body_tmpl: Mayor, I issued a formal Notice of Violation to {BIZ} at {ADDRESS} on {DATE} (case {CASE}). During inspection I found the required secondary exit obstructed by stored inventory and a locked exit door with no panic hardware, both in violation of IFC 1010.1.10 and 1031.2. With the assembly occupancy load posted at the front, this is a serious egress concern. I gave a 10-day correction deadline and advised the owner that continued occupancy above the posted load will not be permitted until corrected. Re-inspection is scheduled. I will notify the building official if non-compliance continues.
reply_tmpl: Egress is non-negotiable. Keep the pressure on and let me know if it heads toward an enforcement action.

body_tmpl: Mayor, following up on case {CASE}: I re-inspected {BIZ} at {ADDRESS} on {DATE} and confirmed all cited violations have been abated. The blocked egress is clear, panic hardware was installed per IFC 1010.1.10, and the extinguishers are now tagged and current under NFPA 10. The owner completed the corrections ahead of the deadline. I am closing the case as compliant and a certificate of compliance will issue. No further action needed.

Mayor, the re-inspection of {BIZ} at {ADDRESS} on {DATE} (case {CASE}) failed. The sprinkler system remains out of certification, two of the three cited egress deficiencies are uncorrected, and the owner has not engaged a contractor despite the original 30-day deadline. This is the second missed deadline. Per IFC 109, I am recommending referral to the village attorney for administrative adjudication and possible daily fines beginning at {AMOUNT}. I'd like your concurrence before I escalate. The occupancy remains open pending the legal process, but I am monitoring closely.
reply_tmpl: Concur on the referral. Send the file to the attorney and copy the village manager.

body_tmpl: Mayor, during the annual inspection of the {BIZ} warehouse at {ADDRESS} on {DATE} (inspection {CASE}) I found high-piled storage exceeding 12 feet with sprinkler coverage not rated for the commodity class, contrary to IFC Chapter 32 and the design basis under NFPA 13. Storage had crept above the original permit limits. I issued a Notice of Violation requiring either reduced pile height or a hydraulic re-evaluation of the system within 30 days. This is a meaningful fire-load concern in the industrial corridor. The owner is consulting their sprinkler contractor. Will update after re-inspection.
reply_tmpl: Stay on it. The industrial corridor is exactly where we can't afford a coverage gap.

body_tmpl: Mayor, inspection of {BIZ} at {ADDRESS} on {DATE} (case {CASE}) identified an electrical fire hazard: an open junction box, daisy-chained power strips serving fixed equipment, and a blocked electrical panel with less than the 36-inch clearance required under IFC 605. I issued an immediate correction order for the panel clearance and a 15-day deadline for the wiring, and recommended the owner retain a licensed electrician. No power shutoff was necessary. Coordinating with the building official on the permit side. Sharing for your awareness given prior complaints at this address.

Mayor, a complaint-driven inspection of {BIZ} at {ADDRESS} on {DATE} (case {CASE}) found the assembly area operating well above its posted occupant load with the second exit partially obstructed, in violation of IFC 1004 and 1031. I directed the manager to reduce occupancy on the spot and issued a Notice of Violation. Given this is a nightlife venue, I recommend coordination with the police liaison on weekend capacity checks. Re-inspection is scheduled and I will recompute the posted load against the current layout. Flagging given the corridor and the complaint history.
reply_tmpl: Good call pulling PD in. Please send me the corrected occupant load once you've recomputed it.

body_tmpl: Mayor, here is the second-quarter commercial inspection summary for your packet. The bureau completed scheduled annual inspections across retail, restaurant, multifamily, and industrial occupancies, with the majority passing on first inspection. A handful generated Notices of Violation, most commonly for egress obstructions, extinguisher service lapses under NFPA 10, and alarm/sprinkler certification gaps. All re-inspections are tracking against deadlines and one case at {ADDRESS} (case {CASE}) was referred for adjudication. Inspection fees collected this quarter totaled {AMOUNT}. Full address-level detail is attached. Happy to brief the board if useful.
reply_tmpl: Numbers look solid. Put a short version in the next board digest.

subject_tmpl: Fire plan review APPROVED — new build-out, {BIZ} at {ADDRESS}
body_tmpl: Mayor, the fire department plan review for the {BIZ} tenant build-out at {ADDRESS} (permit {CASE}) is approved. The submitted sprinkler shop drawings conform to NFPA 13, the fire-alarm device layout meets NFPA 72 spacing, and the egress design satisfies IFC Chapter 10 for the proposed occupancy. I have one condition: the contractor must schedule a rough-in and a final acceptance test with my office before the certificate of occupancy is issued. Comments have been transmitted to the building official and the applicant. No action needed from you — sharing since this is a new business in the corridor.
reply_tmpl: Good to see new tenants moving in. Thanks for the quick review.

body_tmpl: Mayor, I have completed first-round plan review on the mixed-use development by {BIZ} at {ADDRESS} (permit {CASE}) and returned comments to the design team. The submittal needs a standpipe system for the floor area and height under IFC 905, the fire-alarm riser diagram is missing required notification appliance coverage per NFPA 72, and fire-department access road dimensions fall short of the 26-foot aerial-apparatus width in IFC Appendix D. None of these are unusual for a project this size; I expect them resolved on resubmittal. Sharing for awareness given the development's visibility.

subject_tmpl: Plan review pending — above-ground fuel tank, {BIZ}, {ADDRESS}
body_tmpl: Mayor, the plan review for the proposed above-ground fuel storage tank at the {BIZ} fleet facility, {ADDRESS} (permit {CASE}), is on hold pending additional information. The applicant must provide secondary containment sizing and tank separation distances per IFC Chapter 57 and NFPA 30, and the emergency venting and spill-control details are incomplete. I have requested a revised site plan. Once resubmitted I anticipate a straightforward approval. No board action required at this time — flagging because fuel-storage permits sometimes draw neighbor questions.
reply_tmpl: Thanks. Give me a heads-up if any neighbors call so my office isn't caught flat-footed.

subject_tmpl: Hydrant flow test results — Northwest district (NFPA 291)
body_tmpl: Mayor, the bureau completed this year's hydrant flow testing in the northwest district per NFPA 291, covering the next segment of our five-year cycle. Most hydrants met or exceeded the target fire flow and were color-coded accordingly. Static and residual pressures were within expected range. Three hydrants flowed below 500 GPM and have been marked red pending coordination with the water department. The full test record with locations is attached for the file (record {CASE}). This data also feeds our ISO rating and pre-incident planning. No action needed beyond the Public Works coordination, which I am handling.
reply_tmpl: Thanks, Chief. Make sure Public Works has the three low-flow locations in writing.

subject_tmpl: Hydrant OUT OF SERVICE — {STREET} & {CROSS}, bagged pending repair
body_tmpl: Mayor, the hydrant at {STREET} and {CROSS} has been tagged and bagged out of service as of {DATE} (record {CASE}) after a flow test revealed a broken main valve. The water department has been notified and estimates repair within the week. In the interim, our pre-incident plans reflect the nearest in-service hydrants on the adjacent blocks, and dispatch has been advised so apparatus assignments account for the impairment per NFPA 25. This is a routine impairment, not a coverage emergency, but I want it on your radar in case a resident inquires.

subject_tmpl: Hydrant impairment list — current out-of-service units
body_tmpl: Mayor, here is the current hydrant impairment list for your awareness. As of {DATE} we have several units out of service: two bagged for broken valves, one struck by a vehicle at {ADDRESS}, and one with low flow pending a water-main repair. Each is tracked under NFPA 25 impairment procedures, flagged in dispatch, and reflected in our pre-incident plans so crews know the nearest alternate. The water department has the repair list and target restoration dates. None of the impairments leave a block without coverage, but I update this list as units come back online.
reply_tmpl: Appreciate the rundown. Keep me current if any of these drag past their target dates.

body_tmpl: Mayor, the bureau has completed an updated pre-incident plan for {BIZ} at {ADDRESS} (file {CASE}), one of our target-hazard occupancies. The plan documents building layout, sprinkler and standpipe connections, utility shutoffs, hazardous-materials storage locations, hydrant placement, and apparatus access, consistent with NFPA 1620. The update was triggered by a change in the building's storage use and a new addition. The plan is loaded to our mobile units so responding crews have it on arrival. No action required — filing for the record and your awareness given the site's size and occupancy.

Mayor, {BIZ} at {ADDRESS} has completed its Knox-box installation and fire-alarm monitoring registration with the bureau (registration {CASE}) as a condition of its certificate of occupancy. The rapid-entry key vault is installed at the main entrance per village ordinance and IFC 506, and the alarm panel is now reporting to a UL-listed central station per NFPA 72. This eliminates forced-entry damage on alarm responses and gives crews keyed access to the riser room. Registration fee of {AMOUNT} was collected. Routine item — sharing so the record reflects the new business is fully set up for emergency response.
reply_tmpl: Good — the Knox-box program has saved us a lot of broken doors. Thanks for closing this out.
